column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Travertine Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/stone/natural/travertine-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Travertine surface cleaning without thermal damage
Near-IR wavelength optimized for Travertine absorption and minimal substrate damage
Beam spot diameter for balanced cleaning efficiency and resolution
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and process speed
Fluence level sufficient for contaminant removal below Travertine damage threshold
Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal with controlled thermal penetration
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning without overlapping damage
Number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap for complete coverage without excessive energy deposition

Typical range for pulsed Nd:YAG lasers (1064 nm) on porous stones like travertine to remove contaminants without thermal damage; starting at 50 W for light soiling, max 200 W risks microcracking (source: IPG Photonics laser cleaning guidelines and studies on limestone ablation, e.g., J. Cult. Herit. 2018).

Effective speeds balance fluence and coverage; 1000 mm/s optimal for uniform cleaning on travertine, slower (500 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (up to 3000 mm/s) for light dust (source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe and equipment manuals like those from CleanLaser).

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning with minimal heat-affected zone on heat-sensitive travertine; 20 ns balances ablation efficiency and reduced thermal diffusion (picosecond/fs for ultra-precision but overkill; source: Laser physics research in Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020 on stone heritage cleaning).

Typical for industrial cleaning to achieve pulse overlap without excessive heat buildup in low-conductivity travertine; 30 kHz recommended for faster processing vs. heat risks at higher rates (source: Academic literature, e.g., Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019 on pulsed laser parameters for carbonates).

Fluence range for contaminant removal (e.g., soot, oxides) on travertine; damage threshold ~2-3 J/cm² for CaCO3-based stone, so 1.0 J/cm² safe starting point to avoid spalling (source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Appl. Phys. 2017 and heritage conservation papers).

Beam diameter for effective energy distribution; 200 μm recommended for travertine to cover pores without pinpoint damage, smaller for fine details, larger for broad areas (source: Equipment specs from manufacturers like Raycus and Coherent laser systems).

Multi-pass approach ensures complete removal without exceeding fluence limits on delicate travertine; 2 passes typical for industrial surface prep (single pass for very light contamination; source: Industry case studies in stone restoration, e.g., Restaurators 2021).

Ensures uniform coverage and prevents stripe patterns on uneven travertine surfaces; 70% optimal for balance between efficiency and overlap-induced heating (source: Process optimization in laser scanning studies, e.g., Opt. Express 2016).
